verdict_reason: Both disagreements (prajñām scope; anuvidhīyate as passive directionality) are substantive, not nominal. (1) The prajñām disagreement names a real doctrinal axis: Shankara reads it as ātma-anātma-viveka (Advaita technical term); Ramanuja reads it as the aspirant's discrimination directed toward the Lord (Vishishtadvaita); Prabhupada and Mukundananda flatten to 'intelligence' / 'intellect'. The explanation correctly identifies that the metaphor (wind-boat) is preserved identically across all four but the technical sense of prajñā varies. (2) The anuvidhīyate disagreement is grammatically substantive — the verb is passive, the mind is the subject being led, not the leader. The English translations preserve the directionality but compress to 'focuses on' (active framing), losing the passivity that Shankara's gloss preserves. The explanation correctly notes 'this matters: the verse is naming a passivity-failure, not an active grasping' — which is exactly the doctrinal force of the passive verb-form. Both explanations would survive Sanskrit-grammarian scrutiny. Pass.
